Hey, Tyler.
How are you, man?
I'm doing great.
I'm doing great.
How's things in Chicago?
Uh, snowy, cold, snowy, um, gonna get colder.
Uh, so, what, you're down in Houston?
Yeah, we've got, like, the opposite, right?
Like, it's way too hot in the summer over here.
It's perfect in the summer over there, although it's been getting hotter the last couple years, I've heard.
Um, but, fun fact, my first internship, uh, I was living across the street from the Chicago Theater.
I was working at PwC, but I was right in the heart of the city.
Yeah.
Okay.
Yeah.
I mean, I would, that's where, that was my kind of first internship too.
I was at ESPN Radio across the street from the Chicago Theater at the ABC building.
So that was my, my first, you know, commute to the city and getting over to their, to their station too.
So small world.
Very cool.
Yeah.
I was at the School of the Art Institute dorms, but I do like remember that ABC building, I feel like at the corner.
Yeah.
I mean, it changed a lot.
I was going there in 2005, 2006.
But yeah, it's like right at the corner of, I think, State and Lake and the Chicago Theater's right there.
And I mean, I was, it was an ESPN Radio.
I wasn't doing anything.
I was, you know, getting lunches in the mid-January, mid-February for all the, all the radio.
Guys, they'd all send me in a million different directions as well.
So it would be 10 degrees outside because somebody wanted Chipotle and somebody wanted this and somebody wanted that.
So, but yeah, that's a small world that you were kind of hanging around there for your first out-of-school experience as well.
Yeah, I'm sure those guys are cautiously optimistic about the Bears this year at ESPN Radio.
Oh, yeah, yeah.
Yeah, I mean, I'm not really a Bears fan, but yeah, I mean, that's all that they talk about.
And I mean, they haven't been, not only have they not been good in a long time, they haven't had like this kind of like actual momentum and buzz in years.
So yeah, it is 24-7 Bears talk around here for sure.
That's awesome.
Well, great to meet you.
My name's obviously Tyler.
I'm the founding AE here at GrowthX.
Been here about four or five months.
And...
And goal for the conversation today is like, really just want to get to know you.
We're a sales team of one.
And naturally, we'd be working really close together.
And so just get to know you personally, get to know your background.
And then I've got some questions that, you know, we're running through with every candidate.
But the first thing I just wanted to hear a little bit about is, you know, just like, tell me about your journey to sales and a couple of like those moments, right?
Like why you went to different places and also like why you think GrowthX could be a compelling next opportunity.
Yeah.
Oh, man.
I'll try to be brief.
I don't know how deep in the weeds.
And I'm gonna say, you know, if you want to know more, but sales was kind of an accident.
I'll be honest, like if we're going back to like when I first started, it actually was like I was at ESPN radio as an intern graduating.
And one of the hosts was like, hey, man, I'm sure we'd get you a job if you want to stay on here.
Do you know what you want to do?
What's your plan?
I mean, I didn't know.
I was, you know, 22 years old.
I was like, I don't, you know, you know, I don't know.
That's why I did consulting.
Yeah, same thing.
I had no idea.
I was like, I don't know.
And he was like, hey, like, so obviously being here, like we know a lot of people, the different, you know, sports teams.
Like I can call the guy over at the Chicago Bulls.
Like he's kind of got this like intern program where they're selling season tickets and group tickets to folks.
And it's like a, it's a great program.
He's a great guy.
And like, I can get you an in, I can get you an interview, you know?
And I was like, okay, like getting a job with the Bulls, like, you know, grown up here, Michael Jordan, like, sure.
Like, like, so yeah, long story short, that was kind of how it started.
Like I got that internship.
It was like nine, 10 months long and you're selling season tickets and group tickets.
So like, that was like the first like sales.
Yeah.
Where it sort of accelerated and took off was, is, was in the.
Groupon world was in that startup world was in that environment where you're out of sports, you're realizing there's more money when you get out of sports.
And then there's just that whole like environment of like jumping on and being early, you know, AE there and being a part of sort of the roller coaster ride that was Groupon.
And then, yeah, just to kind of navigate, you know, from Groupon, wanted more of a B2B opportunity, moved over to Sprout.
As you can imagine, Groupon was very small business heavy and small business focused, at least when I was there.
Was at Sprout for a long, long time as well, worked my way through sort of the ranks there.
That's kind of where I sort of discovered where I started with working with marketers or people in the marketing, you know, departments brought to social media management tools.
So working with marketing managers, social media managers, VPs, and CMOs, depending on size and scope.
And that's kind of where I.
I've actually been, every gig along the way has been selling within the marketing scope.

Definitive Healthcare was the only one that was sales and marketing, but really it was selling healthcare data to marketers who would use that to target certain folks who were looking to target hospital systems and cardiologists and physicians and things like that.
But, so yeah, I've been in the marketing world, the metadata world, which might be a little bit similar to at least growth, at least there might be some crossover.

I actually didn't even realize as I was kind of looking for a new gig and kind of jumping in some opportunities and kind of reading up on that and that the CEO of growth is, he was a metadata, I think, power user.
And he was on our customer board and, and I think he knew a lot of our, our team and leaders.
was like pretty well.
So yeah, metadata was sort of that demand gen and that engine of driving quality leads and doing a multivariate testing.
The last couple of years, some inflection points, just to kind of give you the run through.
Definitive healthcare culturally wasn't a fit.
But it was very, you know, it was also during COVID.
It was a weird time probably for everybody.
Like my wife's a nurse.
I definitely remember feeling like every day she'd go to the hospital and I was always a little bit on edge.
Yeah, just that wasn't maybe culturally a fit, but moved on from there, jumped over to metadata.
Loved my time at metadata.
You know, unfortunately, I think they took on a little too much and there was a couple rounds of layoffs, but cool product, great people.
You know, I had, again, had no envisions of moving on from there.
Just how, how the, how.
But all played out.

And then the last two and a half years, I was at StackAdapt, programmatic, DSP, working, again, sort of the marketing scope a little bit different, but media buyers, ad buyers, but, you know, helping people drive or target an audience, drive people to take action on said product or said service.
So, and I think what excites me about growth, and then I'm happy to kind of go back into anything there, Tyler, that you want me to expand on.
I think it's really like, you know, the obvious cliche thing is obviously AI, right?
Like, that's so corny to say, like, but like, this is like where the world is going.
This is the world we live in.
you know, from a personal standpoint, figuring out how to use it from things at home for the family and myself, but obviously in the working world, like, it's, it's, it's not going anywhere.
And it's, there are definitely products that caught my eye and from how I.

Gathered this one is, and I also interpreted this as like, I've sold agencies, I've also competed against agencies, like there is like an opportunity here for certain companies to thrive because they are going to consolidate headcount.
They're going to, you know, they're going to allow people to bring things in-house.
You know, when you think about the state of not only companies, brands, individuals, I'm kind of lumping individuals in here, but maybe you'll get where I'm going.

Like, it's a very content-driven world we're in, we're living in right now, and people are, whether you're an individual trying to be the, consistently posting and be on brand with your, whatever you're providing, or obviously a company having not only a strong presence, but a presence that you have the ability to create that message and deliver it constantly and have quality and value.
Like, I'm sure like an everyday person.
I would have a hard time coming up with that stuff and delivering it in all different outlets and facets that people are trying to do.

And obviously something like growth is accelerating that and making it a lot easier from, again, from my research that you now can potentially leave an agency if you're paying one, who I know from, again, selling to them and against them that there is some heavy fees depending on who you bring on.

And then just from a content perspective and a headcount perspective, not that I want to say that it's going to eliminate jobs, but if you don't need to have 12 people on a team, maybe you need to have three or four, that also probably is very appealing to a company depending on where they're at in their growth.
So I guess maybe pun intended there.
But sorry, hopefully that gave you a little bit of a path.
I'm happy to go back and expand anything that you had some follow-up questions on.
Yeah.
Yeah, absolutely.
Pretty open-ended by design, but just sort of wanted to hear your story and different experiences that you've been through.
I think what's cool at GrowthX is everybody is trying to figure out how to leverage AI to create content that shows up in AI search.
And we've got a nice approach that allows us to create quality pretty quickly.
So in the company's brand, written in a way that's to their target audience, but also at a scale where they can publish daily.
And really, with LLMs, it's how can you answer every part of the audience's question?
Not just targeting one keyword, but there's an example where you put it in a search and there's seven different citations that show up from GrowthX.
So like seven pieces of content we created just for like that one search.
So it's cool.
It's like the best of AI and...
Experts, like SEO, content marketing experts.

Since you've been more like on the paid and social media side, I guess just like tell me kind of high level, you know, what you've seen in terms of the importance in investing in organic content, like website content versus like what you've sold in the past, right?

Like you've sold paid, you've sold social media, like I guess just like tell me a little bit about your perspective of like there's these different places you can invest, like, you know, what are the benefits to like the ones that you've sold versus the one that we sell here?
Yeah, I mean, it's all, first of all, it's all connected, right?
Like it's all, it's all message, right?

Like the message needs to align across the board, whether it's a social post or a paid post, like, you know, ultimately everybody that I've, you know, in my however many years of doing this, and selling a market, everybody wants traffic, everybody wants traffic to the website, whether it's paid or organic.
And the more
The more that you can deliver people to your site to take action on something, however you're able to do that, the better, right?
Like I would be like social media management, it's organic, right?
Like it was, you know, I remember being in conversations, whether it was a nonprofit or it was a sports team, everything is, we need people to take action.
So it's either buying tickets, season tickets, nonprofit, you know, believing in our cause, donating, you know, working at programmatic, obviously spending big bucks on advertisements.
It's all to take action on their site, fill out a form, you know, become a customer, become a lead.
But that messaging has to align, right?
It has to be clean, has to be concise.

So, you know, they, I mean, one of the big conversations we would have in any of them is, especially on the programmatic side, was like, hey, you know, I'm looking at your site now, like, where are we sending these people?
Like, you know, how?
How are they gonna navigate what you want them to do, right?
Hey, we would say, like, I think there's too many clicks here to fill out this form.
Like, how do we, you know, either build a landing page or make it more seamless to get them to take action?
You know, one of them was, again, Carfax, like the, you know, like, we want people to, like, we need less clicks, right?
We want people on your site, but like, if you want them to fill out a form, but the messaging still has to align, right?
There was a nonprofit that I worked with in, you know, it was the Central Park Conservatory.
Like, we need people, you, that message also needs to be on your website, you know, and it needs to, like, so it all is all part of it.
And it is, I don't know what you, what we share, but like, I've done a little bit of knowledge gain as well of like, you know, more people are using AEOs.
To get their information, right?
Like people are skipping and they're going to chat GPT or perplexity or Gemini or I can't even think of, there's probably some, I don't even, I can't even think of right now.
haven't discovered.
Claude, mean, you got them, you got most of them, yeah.
Yeah, but within there, like, I know that there's a formula and I'm sure your team is aware of it as well that like drives the content to show up there.
And it's not just like put it and it's going to be picked up.
Like there's that, you know, there's got to be some, like a strong headline.
There's got to be some recency to it.
It's got to, you know, have an authority within the space so that people go there and they know that this is like, you know, an article that they would want to source from.
Like it's not, there's, I've learned that there's more to it than just, create content and it's going to get picked up there.
Like there's things that the marketer can do, or I'm guessing growth acts can do and the team behind it.

To say, hey, it's not just putting out content and that's great and I hope it's relevant, but it's like as the world is shifting, we need to make sure that you're, depending on who the company is, your article, your brand, your reputation is also being cited and sourced within these engines.
Yeah, absolutely.
It's like a really nice blend of art and science.
The science is like the keyword analysis and the competitor content, the search volume, the difficulty of ranking ahead of your competitor.
The art is how do you create the best answer to that audience's question, right?
How do you choose the right content assignment and do better research or deeper research or agentic research where it's going to happen in loops until it hits certain thresholds that you define.
And so like we take a lot of the hard work of trying to think through how do you build a workflow like that, where most people just get stuck with, you know, a custom GPT.
They're trying to, like, one-shot, you know, most of the content creation process.
We've got a, you know, way that separates every piece of that, right?
Generating a brief, coming up with an assignment, doing deep research, drafting an outline, drafting an article, fact-checking that article.
Getting into, you know, great answer on, like, the marketing landscape.
I totally agree.
It's all connected.
One thing I'd love to hear is, like, at one of these companies where you were selling, you know, to marketing leaders, how did you, like, position your product's value in one of those deals?
It could be recent.
It could be whatever you want.
Yeah.
I mean, a few examples.
I'll try to be brief.
But, like, you know, the first example that always comes to mind is, it was a while ago, but it was my biggest deal when I was at Sprout Social.
I had worked with Coinbase, the cryptocurrency company, and that deal...
They're probably just starting to blow up, too, right?
Yeah, and I didn't even know what crypto was.
I fell on my sword there.
I was like, you're going to have to explain this to me.
I like, I have no idea what this is.
But at first, it just wanted to be, hey, we have this platform for buying and selling coins or whatever.
And we're just trying to assess it from a social care standpoint.
People are trading and they have questions.
They want them to interact with us and our team will respond.
And our tool was able to do that and keep track and so they can score their internal employees.
So that's how it started.
then it evolved into, you know, social listening and what are the people saying about Coinbase and the crypto world and the coin, the individual coin.
So like, then we kind of shifted to like, are you aware of these conversations?
And so, I mean, with them getting up to their CMO was like, well, what do you want to be perceived as in the social world?
As this takes off and you guys take off, like, what are you trying to understand?
And it was for them, from a content perspective, wanted from two things, from a crisis.
They wanted to be out ahead of anything going wrong from a coin perspective or just how people are using Coinbase.
And then two, it was easier for them to understand future content if they knew how the world was perceiving the crypto world and the Coinbase world.
Right.
Like they were missing out on all those conversations or it would have been a lot more manual of a process had they not added on like our social listening and like different terms.

I think they bought like 25 like terms where they can change or use hashtags and, you know, Boolean searches, which, you know, this is a while ago, but like to make sure that they weren't missing it because that would impact, you know, future opportunities, whether it was paid or organic as well.
So the value is just like, if you don't do this, how is your team going to know, like, as you were taking off now, it's obviously six, six years later.
It's huge, you know, like seven years later, it's even weirder than it was then.
Like, how are you going to know, like, how are you going to adjust if you're not being, if you don't know how you're being perceived in this world that's taking off?
So that became kind of like a little bit of a no-brainer at the time.
The longevity of the deal was just more legal.

So once we kind of bought in and we went through some terms and the conversations that were being had and they started seeing where they were populating the bad, the good and the bad, you know, that was like instant, you know, value for them because the, you know, the, the hands on keyboard stuff, you know, she didn't care about, but like she wanted to know those other things pretty quickly as they popped up.
And then the other deal was Carfax at StackAdapt, so a little more recent.
But the value was just like, they had no control over the story because they were paying an agency.
They, so they wanted, you know, an ROI factor immediately.
It was like, hey, we don't want to pay somebody.
We're not necessarily seeing results.
We want more control over our story.
And we're just an old school, believe it or not, I couldn't believe it.
were like a three-person team, right?
It was just like three gals doing paid media.
And I would have thought like Carfax would have had this huge, maybe 10-person team doing stuff.
So they were stretched thin.
So then it was our managed services.
So we were saving them there because they were going to spend enough.
Content and creatives, if you spend enough, we also were throwing those in.
It made our platform sticky.
And so we just like became a huge like pain relief from a financial and a creative standpoint for them because they didn't have to do any of that stuff anymore or pay a bunch of money for it.
So the value to them, to their VP of marketing was like, you know, immediately time saved.
And we had all the data and the results that would guarantee more impressions and clicks and engagements because we had.
The creative team that had all the, you know, data on what makes a successful campaign and what makes a successful creative campaign.
Yeah, it makes a lot of sense.
Those are two pretty cool logos to close to.
With the Coinbase example, like what metrics or outcomes did you use to connect like Sprout Social to their growth strategy?
Like what metrics or outcomes did you use on Coinbase?
Yeah, so on, so it started off with social care.

So initially it was just like, we need to know like CSAT scores or NPS, like the things that like connected the time to a, their social care people responding in a reasonable amount of time if somebody came in with a customer issue.
So that's how that started.
As it evolved into more listening and more analytics, it turned into the sentiment in social listening tools, so positive and negative sentiment.
into sentiment and then the volume.
The of that and the impressions, so it'd be like, if it was negative, what was the volume that was potentially being perceived out there that was connecting the dots and the impact for them?
So like, call it a little bit of crisis management.
They didn't want anything to get out ahead that they weren't aware of.
As far as like general, you know, day-to-day, it was more of just like, mean, social media management is a little bit more generic.
It was mainly impressions and that people were clicking and engaging with their content, so it was clicks.
We had an integration with Bitly at the time and, you know, CTAs on their, excuse me, CTAs on their links.

And then we integrated with their Google Analytics account to sort of connect those dots for them that if someone, if they were promoting a coin or a promotion or to take action, that they can connect those dots on the, just a general marketing side.
Yeah, it's a really interesting.

Use case of Sprout Social, and I like what you said about, you know, the crisis management, but also, like, that world was changing so fast, and if they could see in real time how people were perceiving, you know, you can almost paint this incredible narrative and story about what opportunity could be unlocked from those kind of insights, you know?

Well, yeah, that's why it's like, once we put in, like, I think we just put in initially, we, some of my SE and I, we built, like, a custom demo environment, and just to present to them, and then I remember being on the call, they gave us some coins, and they gave us some hashtags, but, like, we just put in, like, hashtag Coinbase, right?
And then we put in the two or three main coins, and just, like, the volume, and you could actually see the messages, and you could see, like, the complete perception of what's going on.

And you could see if the person had a huge following or a small following, so, like, it allowed them to make sure, like, if somebody who had, you know, I guess it was the early days of, you know, influencers, like, who was...
team is responding to people's complaints and questions.
And then it just evolved.
The deal took about a year of my life.
Sounds like, did y'all get a case study out of it or anything?
Sounds like it's an amazing story to tell.
I don't know.
I don't think so because I think we signed an NDA and I think that they didn't want anybody to know what they were doing.
And we were in legal for like three and a half months.
Yeah.
At the end, I was just...
Crypto is tricky.
Crypto is tricky, you know, especially at that time.
It was like all, we'll be around in a year.
Yeah.

And I was just literally at the end just quarterbacking our legal team and their legal team to get on a call and stop messing through email and like, let's like break this, this, you know, these, these T's and C's down.
Because we weren't getting anywhere when it was just like via email.
Like, but yeah, the last couple of months were literally just getting them to agree to these calls to get the stuff taken care of.
Like, you know, they wanted the moon, the stars in the sky.
Obviously, we had our limits on what we were going to give.

And, but eventually, you know, we, it was like, I think, you know, at the time, this was, our deals were, sizes were like, 10K, 12K, 15K, 20K, as we were getting more sophisticated, this was like 90K.
So at the time, this was like, you know, a big deal in the social media world for us.
Definitely, definitely.
What do you feel like was your like, biggest personal accomplishment in that example, in particular?

Yeah, mean, honestly, just the cliche is just like sticking with my champion, like sticking based on, you know, as we, the marketing manager kind of kicked off with him and I, and it was just more of like, as we went through all these different rounds of how we started with social care, and then it expanded, and it turned into social listening, just like making sure that him and I were on the same page as far as like, buy-in, you know, he was my champion for this.
You know, which obviously share things with how to like, navigate, navigate certain person.
So like, and this is kind of like, I mean, you know, like using their words against them, but not, you know what I mean?
Like, hey, you said you wanted this, this was important to you, so this was something that you think would, you know, benefit you as you, as we checked off those boxes.
And some of them were getting, weren't necessarily initially on their radar.

So yeah, just, just like trying to avoid the happy ears and just thinking about like what they said and having that person sort of buy in and have that back and forth that we can go back and forth on and try to get this done.
So for sure, for sure.
And definitely, I'm sure you had to do a good amount of reassuring and like, you know, when the deal, what do they say, right?
Time kills all deals.
Yeah.
So, so like, yeah, I'm sure you had to, you know, definitely get your champion to stick with it, right?
Because yeah, there was a period of time writing here from him for like two and a half months.
And, you know, but
I just kept using like the value that him and I were talking about to move this through.
And eventually they actually technically for a month to month went to a competitor Hootsuite for like a few months because they just needed something.
Because we were talking about the large scope and they were like, we just need something for the social care.
And I guess selfishly, I guess I didn't want just the social care.
wanted the whole thing after we saw that there was a real value there.
And we just stuck with it.
Like we stuck with it.
We reengaged.
Like, um, I said, could go spend this, but you're missing out on all these other elements of it.
And we just like, um, yeah, just continue to like drive home.

I would send, like, I would continue to go on to their demo instance that we created for them and like, like check keywords and send them the results and the messages and say like, Hey, like, again, that you guys are missing out on this stuff.
Like, and eventually that all just kind of paid off, got back together, you know, kind of regrouped, regrouped on a price point.
And then the last few months were just, like I said, legal, legal conversations.
Makes, makes a lot of sense.
Definitely can relate.
And kudos to you for like personalizing the demo and, you know, letting them actually see how the product would help them specifically.
Like that's something I'm trying to work on here because, you know, a lot of things move quickly and building process is tough when you're also trying to close deals.
So we're building out like more of a demo environment right now, actually.
So it's much needed.
People like to see what they're going to buy, you know.
So I think that's always been the case.
So, yeah, I mean, obviously I learned that at Sprout, like when you can show them like the buttons and what the outcomes are and, you know.
Yeah, it just it just does.
It just like it clicks for people when they can envision themselves doing it and they can see what, you know, what their effort leads to.
And the trap, right, is like you have that and then you start leaning into the features too much.
It's like, yeah, where you got to tell stories, man.
It's it's show tell show, you know, you got to like.
Um.
Um.
Um.
Or tell a story, show it, and then tell another story, the outcome of it.
You try not to get caught up in it, and that's where somebody asks about buttons.
You ask, hey, why did you ask that?
Why is that relevant to you?
You want to make sure they're not just looking at a shiny thing, and then you realize, well, let's focus on what we intended to and not get sidetracked with every little nook and cranny of a tool.
Exactly.
No, I totally agree there as well.
Same example, just kind of like line of questions around it.
What do you feel like was like your biggest failure or like thing you could have done better with that deal?
Coinbase?
Yeah, we're just going super deep on Coinbase.
Yeah, that's fine.
Going back in time.
I know it was a while ago, so take that into account.
In of my life, I definitely remember probably like 97% of it, because I used to come home with my now wife, fiance at the time, was just like, is this done yet?
Is it done yet?
Is it done yet?
And I just like kept talking about it.
Like, we go to...
And she'd be like, okay, let's, we need to take a break from, from Coinbase.
I, you know, easier said than done to play that, you know, hindsight 2020, you know, maybe gotten to power sooner, you know, gotten to, you know, maybe ask for it a little bit sooner in the process.

I definitely remember the first hand, you know, really like realizing it was a good deal, but like maybe spending, once we got enough buy-in on the social care, maybe would have maybe moved to social listening faster, know, gotten to that person faster, you know, the VP of marketing, you know, made more of a case for that sooner, you know, like, and obviously outlined in our demo environment and made sure like, yeah, I think at the time, because it was still going to be a decent sized deal, because it was going to be still very user-focused and that's how Sprout was, like it was very user-based and, you know, maybe if I would have just like, you know, again, but like just.
Tabled that and started asking more broader questions earlier.

I could have gotten to it sooner and I could have like maybe got out ahead of some of, you know, the lull that we had and maybe wouldn't have had a regroup because there was a period of time I was like, oh, crap, I don't know.
Maybe I'm not going to get this back.
Like, even though we stuck true, you know, like sometimes you just don't know, like you don't know what's happening on the other end.
Yeah, it's kind of interesting, right?

Because there's like this, you know, tradeoff between I stick with my champion and I don't frustrate them and like maybe like they introduce that buyer when they feel really confident and then it comes with more momentum.
Then there's also like how much power does this person have?
Are they actually a champion?
Should I just like cut my losses and try to get access to them with the value prop that I have sooner?
And even just explain to the champion like why that's in their best interest.
But hindsight's 20-20 for sure.
And I've definitely gotten stuck with like, you know, manager level more than I.
